title: What can't GDP see?
tension: The single number that decides budgets, credit ratings, and front-page headlines is blind to almost everything that makes a life good or bad. It cannot see who got the money, whether the air is breathable, whether the river still runs, or whether the woman who cooked three meals and raised two children produced anything at all. By the official count, she didn't. A country can post record GDP growth while its air poisons its children, its inequality widens, and its groundwater vanishes, and the number will not flinch. Knowing what GDP cannot see is the difference between reading it wisely and being ruled by it.
teachingGoal: Take the reader past the headline to GDP's blind spots, one at a time and in depth: distribution, unpaid work, the environment, and the hard-to-measure informal economy. They should understand the mechanism behind each blind spot, see a concrete Indian example, learn what alternative measures exist (and why none has replaced GDP), and finish with a short, practical list of what to read alongside GDP for an honest picture.
sectionArc: GDP measures output. So what is it blind to? (set up the four big blind spots)
It cannot tell you who got the money (distribution; the average above the median; record growth alongside widening inequality)
It ignores the work that holds society together (unpaid care and domestic work; the production boundary; the gendered silence)
It counts cutting the forest, not losing it (the environment; natural-capital depletion and pollution are not subtracted; 'green GDP' and environmental accounts)
It struggles to even see the informal economy (most Indian work is informal; estimated, not measured; why this makes the number fragile)
If GDP is this blind, why do we still use it? (it is the best single comparable number we have; the alternatives, HDI and others, and why none replaced it)
What should you read alongside GDP? (per-capita and median income, an inequality measure, the Human Development Index, environmental and employment data: a short honest-picture reading list)
mustTeach: GDP measures production, and is silent on distribution: it can rise while inequality widens
unpaid care and domestic work fall outside the production boundary and are invisible in GDP, a heavily gendered omission
GDP does not subtract environmental damage or the depletion of natural capital
much of India's economy is informal and estimated rather than measured, making the number fragile
alternatives like the Human Development Index and green-GDP accounts exist but none has replaced GDP as the headline

contextCards.fact: Where GDP draws its 'production boundary' is a convention, not a law of nature. A meal cooked and sold in a restaurant counts; the same meal cooked at home does not. Feminist economists, notably Marilyn Waring, showed that this leaves most of women's unpaid care and domestic work invisible in the headline number. In India, where women do a large share of unpaid domestic and care work, that silence is especially loud.
contextCards.source: Marilyn Waring, 'If Women Counted' (1988); SNA production boundary (standard)

contextCards.fact: GDP does not subtract the using-up of nature: when a mine is exhausted or a river polluted, the output is counted but the loss is not. The UN's System of Environmental-Economic Accounting (SEEA) lets countries build 'satellite accounts' that value natural resources and their depletion, and economists have long proposed a 'green GDP' that nets out environmental damage. No major economy has adopted one as its headline number.
contextCards.source: UN System of Environmental-Economic Accounting (SEEA); standard environmental-economics literature

contextCards.fact: The UN's Human Development Index (HDI) combines income with life expectancy and years of schooling to capture wellbeing that output alone misses. India ranks far lower in the world on HDI than it does by the sheer size of its GDP, a gap that stands for everything the production number leaves out about health and education.
contextCards.source: UNDP Human Development Index (standard, widely established)

contextCards.fact: A large share of India's economy is informal and unregistered (the street vendor, the unwritten-contract carpenter, the family farm), so the national accounts estimate it rather than measure it directly, often using formal-sector or corporate data as a proxy. This makes the headline both incomplete and fragile, especially when a shock hits the informal economy harder than the formal one.
